title: Rubin-Class Shift and Its Implications for AI Infrastructure | by elongated_musk | Medium
text:
Rubin-Class Shift and Its Implications for AI Infrastructure
Rubin-class GPUs will force a step-change in data center design. They promise unprecedented performance per node — but only if fed by equally advanced memory and network fabrics, and only where power and cooling provisions are scaled to match. Capacity planners face a pivotal choice: retrofit existing sites for short-term gains, or invest in new “AI factories” ready to unlock Rubin’s full potential. The calculus hinges on timing, cost of capital, and one’s confidence that memory and interconnect technologies will arrive on schedule to deliver Rubin’s expected leap.
- Memory Jump: Rubin-class GPUs are expected to debut High Bandwidth Memory 4 (HBM4), roughly doubling per-chip bandwidth and capacity over Blackwell’s HBM3e. This enables single accelerators with ≥ 0.5 TB of fast memory at ~15+ TB/s bandwidth, though at a significant cost premium (30%+ per bit vs HBM3e). Memory pooling via CXL 3.0 is emerging as a complementary strategy for large inference models, trading bandwidth for lower $/GB.
- Interconnect & IO: NVIDIA’s fifth-gen NVLink (in Blackwell) already delivers 1.8 TB/s GPU-to-GPU bandwidth. A Rubin-class NVLink (gen 6) is anticipated to double this (~3.6 TB/s per GPU), likely necessitating an optical 1.6 Tb/s network to avoid bottlenecks at rack scale. Industry roadmaps point to 102.4 Tb/s switches with 64 × 1.6 Tb ports by 2025, shrinking oversubscription targets (aiming ≈1:1 for training clusters). Early adopters should budget for ~25% higher node interconnect costs when moving from 800 Gb to 1.6 Tb links.
- Power & Cooling: Rubin-class nodes will push practical rack power from today’s ~50–75 kW into the 150+ kW range. Air cooling alone becomes unviable beyond ~40 kW/rack. Operators are rapidly shifting to liquid cooling: direct-to-chip cold plates or immersion, which cut cooling energy overhead (PUE ~1.07 vs ~1.25 for air-cooled) and make 100 kW+ racks normal. Retrofitting existing facilities can support Rubin-class gear in limited quantities (with rear-door heat exchangers up to ~40 kW/rack), but full-scale deployment often means new or heavily upgraded infrastructure.
- Retrofit vs Greenfield: A retrofit (adding liquid loops to an old data hall) offers faster time-to-deployment — potentially capturing AI revenue 6–12 months sooner — but caps density and efficiency. A greenfield build optimized for >100 kW/rack and <1.10 PUE yields lower long-term $/compute, but demands higher upfront capital and coordination. A breakeven analysis suggests that at ~$0.10/kWh power, a new liquid-cooled facility recovers its higher capital outlay in ~3–5 years via energy savings and capacity for more paying workloads. Operators must weigh the opportunity cost of delay against these savings.
I. Where Blackwell Leaves Us
NVIDIA’s current Blackwell-generation (2024–25) GPUs define the baseline. A single Blackwell GPU (e.g. B100 SXM module) contains 208 billion transistors on TSMC’s 4 nm process, implemented as a dual-die “superchip” linked by a 10 TB/s interposer. At ~700 W TDP per GPU (SXM5 form factor), Blackwell delivers up to 20 PFLOPS of FP8 AI throughput — roughly 2.5× the FP8 performance of its Hopper/H100 predecessor. Each Blackwell carries ~192 GB of HBM3e memory (8 stacks × 24 GB) with an aggregate bandwidth near 8 TB/s. Systems like NVIDIA’s HGX B200 pack 8 such GPUs per board, for ~1.5 PFLOPS FP8 total. In multi-GPU training clusters, Blackwell introduced the 5th-gen NVLink interconnect: ~900 GB/s each direction (1.8 TB/s bidirectional) per GPU. For the first time, an entire 72‑GPU pod can be lashed together as one high-bandwidth domain using NVLink Switch chips (NVL72), yielding up to 13.5 TB of HBM3e pooled memory at 576 TB/s internal bandwidth (cudocompute.com). A full 72-GPU Blackwell pod (as deployed in AWS P6e UltraClusters) draws on the order of 40–50 kW per rack and can deliver ~360 PFLOPS FP8 of compute with 13+ TB of high-speed memory. These figures frame the starting point for “Rubin-class” planning (Table 1).
Table 1 — Hopper vs Blackwell vs Rubin-Class (projected) (Data for shipped products and assumptions for next-gen; sources in Appendix)
Blackwell, in practice, has shipped in two main variants: a 192 GB model (B100) for training and general workloads, and a 384 GB “Ultra” configuration (B200) geared to giant inference models. Both use HBM3e memory running up to ~1 TB/s per stack en.wikipedia.org. It’s worth noting that Blackwell’s efficiency gains also rely on Transformer Engine optimizations and newer data types (FP8/FP4) to reach those headline FLOPS. The net result: compared to Hopper, a Blackwell GPU roughly doubles effective throughput per chip, and a single NVLink domain expands from 8 to 72 GPUs, massively scaling out memory and connectivity within a rack. This context is crucial for judging Rubin-class assumptions.
II. Rubin‑Class Assumptions (and Unknowns)
What do we expect from a “Rubin-class” GPU architecture? While Nvidia has not formally announced a name, the industry consensus (as of late 2025) is that the next major architecture will likely introduce:
- HBM4 Memory — Likely: Yes (vendor-stated). JEDEC’s preliminary HBM4 specs double the interface width to 2048 bits. Early HBM4 samples (Micron, mid-2025) run at 6.4 Gbps/pin for 1.6 TB/s per stack, with 36 GB capacity (12‑Hi) tomshardware.com. Uplift over HBM3e is ~60% bandwidth per stack (and potential for 64 GB stacks later). This is a confirmed trajectory, with SK hynix and Micron both sampling HBM4 in 2025. We assume Rubin GPUs use HBM4 from day one — or offer HBM4 variants — to achieve the needed memory bandwidth.
- Higher Memory Capacity — Likely: Yes (industry rumor). Thanks to HBM4 and possibly more stacks per GPU, Rubin-class cards could approach 0.5–0.8 TB of HBM. Current projections (e.g. TrendForce) indicate 16‑Hi HBM4 stacks of 64 GB are feasibleg. However, capacity per GPU will depend on yield and packaging (more stacks means more interposer area and more power). It’s rumored that Nvidia might offer configurations with say 6–8 HBM4 stacks (for cost-sensitive SKUs) and 12+ stacks on high-memory models.
- Next-Gen NVLink & NVSwitch — Confirmed: In development (vendor-stated). Nvidia’s CTO hinted at “even broader NVLink connectivity” beyond Blackwell’s 72-GPU pod, potentially using NVLink 6 with double the lane speed. If NVLink 5 delivered 1.8 TB/s per GPU, NVLink 6 may target ~3.6 TB/s. It’s unclear if domain size will grow (e.g. >72 GPUs); what’s known is that NVLink Switch System (NVL) will continue to evolve, possibly integrating NVLink with optical links for longer reach. For now, we assume similar pod sizes (≤72 GPUs) but with each GPU’s NVLink bandwidth doubling.
- Node Power & Thermal Design — Likely: Higher (industry observation). Each Blackwell SXM was ~700–800 W; Rubin-class GPUs could push ≳1000 W each. Some projections put a full 8-GPU server at 8–10 kW. We assume a single Rubin GPU module ~≈1 kW TDP, which implies liquid cooling as a must (see Section V). This is partially confirmed by cooling vendors, who are designing cold plates for >1.5 kW per device (e.g. 1.5 kW ASICs).
- PCIe/CXL and Host Connectivity — Confirmed: CXL 3.0 support (vendor-stated). By 2025, Compute Express Link 3.0 is standard for memory pooling and coherent attaches. It’s safe to assume Rubin-class platforms will use PCIe 6.0 / CXL 3.0 interfaces (64 GT/s x16 links, ~256 GB/s non-coherent bandwidth or ~64 GB/s coherent). This would be an upgrade from Blackwell systems that currently use PCIe 5.0 (32 GT/s). Some industry chatter even suggests a shift to NVLink-C2C for coupling Grace CPUs or other accelerators, but in absence of confirmation we assume PCIe 6.
(I label the above “assumptions” as such — they represent the best available public info as of Q3 2025. Dont rely on an of this for anything… The key variables for scenario planning are memory (HBM4) and interconnect (NVLink/optics); if those slip, the Rubin-class launch could under-deliver or be delayed.)
III. Memory Bandwidth & Capacity Implications
Rubin-class GPUs are poised to break the “memory wall” again by moving to HBM4. Table 2 summarizes the step-change:
Table 2 — HBM3e vs HBM4 Quick Look
Even at equal pin speed, HBM4 doubles bandwidth by widening the bus (2048-bit). Early reports show Micron’s 36 GB HBM4 stacks hitting 2 TB/s tomshardware.com, likely using ~7–8 Gbps signals. For a Rubin-class GPU, if we assume 8 stacks, that’s ~16 TB/s on tap — 2× Blackwell’s 8 TB/s. With 12 stacks, it could reach into the mid-20s TB/s, though that might exceed diminishing returns (power and cooling could constrain max achievable bandwidth).
Crucially, this bandwidth does not come cheap. High-end HBM already commands about 5× the cost/GB of DDR5 memory. TrendForce notes HBM4’s complex 3D assembly and logic-based I/O will add >30% price premium. That implies HBM4 might cost on the order of $25–30 per GB in 2025 (vs maybe $20 for HBM3e). Thus a 512 GB HBM4-equipped GPU could carry $12–15k in raw memory cost alone — a major factor in overall server economics.
Memory capacity per GPU, however, becomes less of a bottleneck. With ~0.5 TB on a single card, many inference workloads that used to be spread over multiple 80 GB cards (with slow cross-traffic) can be collapsed. For training, it enables larger model fits per GPU (important for billion-parameter-scale parallelism). Nvidia claims Blackwell’s 192 GB can handle ~740 billion parameter models in-memory; Rubin’s ~512 GB could roughly double that, crossing the trillion-parameter threshold on one node. This will simplify software (less sharding) and reduce all-reduce overheads.
One alternative to huge HBM is emerging: memory pooling via CXL 3.0. The idea is to attach large pools of standard DDR5 or LPDDR memory to the GPU via a coherent CXL link, essentially extending GPU memory at lower bandwidth. For instance, Blackwell’s Grace CPU superchip can access CPU memory at 0.9 TB/s — slower than HBM, but usable for holding giant model weights. A similar concept could be applied with CXL memory expanders. While CXL 3.0 (on PCIe 6.0) tops out around ~64 GB/s per device, future optical CXL or switched CXL fabrics might aggregate bandwidth. The economic crossover comes when HBM $/GB >> pooled DDR5 $/GB and the workload can tolerate lower throughput for a portion of data. For example, an LLM inference serving huge context windows might keep active layers in HBM but spill long-term weights to pooled memory, achieving much higher memory per dollar — at the cost of some latency. By some crude estimates, at 2025 prices, a pooled-DDR solution could offer ~3–4× cheaper memory per GB than HBM4. If the hit on token throughput is minor (say <20%), that can be an attractive trade for inference operators. We expect early experiments in 2025–26 where a Rubin-class GPU is paired with, e.g., 2 TB of CXL memory, to serve massive models more cost-effectively than an all-HBM design. In summary, HBM4 will set new records in memory bandwidth, but clever system architects will augment it with slower-cheaper memory tiers to optimize $/AI token.
IV. Interconnect & Network Fabric
Rubin-class systems will demand an equally extreme interconnect: both on the scale of GPU-to-GPU links (NVLink domains) and rack-to-rack networking. Blackwell raised the bar with NVLink 5 (enabled by NVSwitch 3): any two GPUs in a pod exchange data at 1.8 TB/s cudocompute.com, and a 72-GPU NVL pod has 130 TB/s bisection bandwidth internally. For collective operations (all-reduce, all-gather) in large training jobs, this vastly reduced communication time. Rubin-class (NVLink 6) is expected to double per-GPU link rates again — roughly 3.6 TB/s per GPU of cross-node fabric. Achieving that might entail moving to optical or improved copper tech; Nvidia has hinted at exploring NVLink optical bridging to connect beyond the confines of a single chassis. Even if pod size remains ~72, higher NVLink bandwidth will make those GPUs act even more “as one,” further hiding communication overhead.
On the cluster networking side, the roadmap is clear: 800 Gb/s NICs → 1.6 Tb/s NICs. In 2024, high-end AI clusters use either 8×100 Gb or 16×50 Gb links per node (800 Gb total) via Nvidia Quantum-2 InfiniBand or 800G Ethernet. By late 2025, vendors like Broadcom have launched switch ASICs (Tomahawk 6) to drive 64 ports of 1.6 TbE on a single chip. This aligns with expected Rubin-era requirements: an 8-GPU server could feasibly use dual 1.6 Tb ports (one per 4 GPUs, in a Dragonfly+ topology) or even one 1.6 T per 2 GPUs, to ensure the external fabric doesn’t throttle the now-faster NVLink interconnect. Facebook’s AI research and others have pointed out that to keep scaling efficiency high (≥90%) for large training runs, cluster oversubscription must be minimized — ideally 1:1 for training (each GPU’s network bandwidth ≈ GPU’s internal memory bw * fraction of data needing exchange). With HBM4 at ~15 TB/s and many training tasks exchanging maybe 5–10% of activations, having on the order of 1.6 Tb (0.2 TB/s) per GPU off-node could approach that ideal balance. For inference clusters, a bit of oversubscription is acceptable (commonly 2:1 or 4:1) to save cost, since not all inferencing GPUs communicate heavily at once.
Another consideration is the switch network architecture: today’s fat-tree or Dragonfly networks for AI are expensive and power-hungry. The move to 1.6T optics, plus technologies like co-packaged optics (CPO) in switches, aim to improve power efficiency per bit. A 51.2 Tb switch (32×1.6T) still consumes ~700–800 W, and the upcoming 102.4 Tb gen will be higher. Co-packaged optics can cut losses, but they also complicate upgrades. Many data centers will likely stick with pluggable 1.6 T QSFP-DD or OSFP modules initially, accepting ~25% higher port costs for the generation jump. I guess roughly a +$30k per 8-GPU node increase in networking BOM when moving from 8×100 Gb (P5 era) to dual 1.6 T (Rubin era) connectivity — due to pricier NICs/DPU, optical transceivers, and additional switch capacity. Table 3 illustrates a notional per-node optics cost comparison:
Table 3 — Optics BOM Shift (per 8-GPU server node)
(Costs are illustrative, assuming dual NICs/DPUs per node. Oversubscription is cluster-level ratio of total GPU NVLink bandwidth vs total NIC bandwidth.)
In practice, hyperscalers may adopt 8 × 200 Gb (1.6 Tb) approaches, using distributed vs centralized switching. But regardless of topology, optical IO scales ~linearly with GPU FLOPS in these systems. Datacenter architects should plan power and cooling for much denser optical cable bundles, and ensure that fiber plant (and connectors) can handle 100 Gbps per lane signals. We also see a trend to integrate the NIC functionality on the GPU board (e.g. Nvidia’s Spectrum-X NIC on HGX). By Rubin’s launch, it would not be surprising if a reference design includes on-board 1.6 T optical ports and perhaps tight coupling with the NVLink Switch.
One more fabric element: in-network computing. Blackwell pods introduced Mellanox SHARP in-switch aggregation, effectively giving 7.2 TB/s all-reduce across the NVLink fabric. Future Rubin systems will likely extend this concept — possibly hierarchical: NVSwitch for intra-node, and DPUs or superNICs handling inter-node reduction. This means operators must also budget for next-gen DPUs (e.g. BlueField-4) in each server to offload collective ops and security. These add to cost but help guarantee that a sprawling Rubin-class cluster (potentially 1000s of GPUs) can operate near ideal efficiency. The bottom line: Rubin-class fabric will blur the line between “inside the box” and “outside” — enormous NVLink bandwidth within pods, and 1.6+ Tbit links tying those pods together. The best-prepared facilities will treat the network as equally crucial to performance as the GPUs themselves, with investments in low-latency switches, rich topologies, and careful oversubscription control.
V. Power, Cooling & Facility Envelope
With Rubin-class, we enter an era where a single rack can rival a small substation in power draw. Current high-end AI racks (Blackwell pods, etc.) are already hitting ~50–75 kW. The next jump is 100–200 kW per rack, driven by ~1 kW GPUs, more of them per rack (higher density), and additional network gear. This has profound implications for power delivery and cooling:
Power delivery: Many legacy data halls are designed for <5 kW per rack average. Even retrofitted “high-density” rooms often top out at ~30 kW/rack with augmentation. Rubin-class deployments will require per-rack PDUs and busways capable of 300–400 A feeds (at 415 V 3-phase). It’s likely we’ll see 48 V DC bus designs inside cabinets to reduce losses (some vendors already do this for GPU chassis). Also, operators must consider fault domains — a 150 kW rack losing power abruptly is a significant event. Redundant feeds (dual-corded racks) and on-rack battery UPS (lithium-ion packs) may become standard to manage ride-through and graceful shutdown for these dense racks.
Cooling strategies: Air cooling hits a wall well before 100 kW. — nearly impossible to do efficiently. Thus liquid cooling moves from optional to mandatory for Rubin-class. The typical progression is:
- Air + chilled doors (RDHx): A passive rear-door heat exchanger can remove ~20–40 kW per rack using facility water. This can extend older air-cooled racks a bit further. PUEs ~1.20–1.25 are achievable. But beyond ~50 kW, even RDHx struggles, as remaining heat in-air is still too high.
- Direct-to-chip (cold plate): Here, coolant is piped directly to water blocks on GPUs (and CPUs). This removes ~70–80% of heat at source, leaving only residuals for air introl.com. With 30–45 °C inlet water, many deployments reach <1.10 PUE at rack level. In fact, partial PUEs of 1.02–1.05 for cooling loops have been reported. A modern liquid-cooled rack with warm water cooling can handle 100 kW continuously. Rubin-class nodes (with ~8–10 kW each) are designed for this: e.g. Nvidia’s 72-GPU HGX pods are factory liquid-cooled units. We expect a majority of Rubin installations to use D2C cooling. The main trade-off is upfront cost — cold plate systems and CDUs (coolant distribution units) add perhaps $8K–$12K per rack in infrastructure. But per kW, this is still often cheaper than new air CRAH units for equivalent load.
- Immersion cooling: In extreme cases or for opportunistic reuse of heat, immersion may appear. Single-phase immersion (rack tanks of dielectric fluid) can do >200 kW per rack, but introduces operational complexity (handling heavy fluid-filled enclosures, component service challenges, etc.). Two-phase immersion has even better thermal transfer but uses exotic (expensive, sometimes environmentally problematic) fluids. As of 2025, immersion is still a niche for HPC and crypto. For Rubin-class, immersion could be used in greenfield designs where waste heat recovery is a goal (since high temperature fluids can be used to heat buildings, etc.). Otherwise, direct-to-chip with rear-door assist (a hybrid) likely hits the sweet spot in efficiency vs simplicity.
Facility upgrades: To retrofit an existing data center for 100+ kW racks, one must check floor loading (immersed racks can weigh >2000 kg — some slab floors or raised floors cannot take that), ceiling height and piping (running water manifolds, plus possibly new chillers or dry coolers for added heat), and crucially, power distribution. Often the limiting factor is not cooling but power. You might cool 100 kW, but the site’s upstream transformers or breakers might not handle many such racks. This is why many operators cap retrofit deployments.
We can tabulate typical cooling options and their characteristics (Table 4):
Table 4 — Data Center Cooling Ladder for High-Density
In summary, power/cooling is the make-or-break factor for Rubin-class readiness. Any operator aiming to host these next-gen GPUs at scale must either retrofit with aggressive liquid cooling upgrades or plan new space. The Energy Reuse Factor (ERF) may enter conversations: e.g., a cluster of Rubin GPUs might output 1 MW of heat in a small area — if captured, that could heat office buildings or feed absorption chillers. We note some hyperscalers in cooler climates plan to recycle AI server heat for campus use (ERF >0.5 in some cases, meaning over half the energy reused). This can improve overall economics but requires upfront planning (heat exchangers, pumps, backup cooling for summer).
A final note on operational metrics: expect power densities per square foot to double, but PUE to improve slightly. Many new AI datacenters in 2025 are targeting PUE ~1.10 or below, versus ~1.2–1.3 a few years ago, largely thanks to liquid cooling and higher inlet water temps (less chiller work). Some all-liquid sites even claim PUE ~1.05 on cool days using dry coolers. Rubin-class hardware will thrive in those environments — and conversely, it will suffer if shoehorned into old air-cooled rooms (with risk of thermal throttling or frequent power limit trips).
VI. Retrofit vs Greenfield Economics
Given the above power/cooling needs, operators face a strategic choice: retrofit an existing facility or build new (“greenfield”) for Rubin-class deployments. Each approach has pros/cons, and the decision often hinges on timing and scale:
- Retrofit: adding high-density cooling and power to an existing data center. This might involve installing liquid cooling loops (CDUs, rear-door coolers, etc.), upgrading power feeds to certain racks, and perhaps enhancing containment. The benefit is speed — you might convert a few pods in months, leveraging existing space, and start generating revenue from new GPUs sooner. Upfront cost per MW is lower if the shell and much of the infrastructure is in place (e.g., reusing existing backup generators, switchgear). Perhaps a retrofit for Rubin-class (to support ~60–80 kW racks) might cost on the order of $5–8 million per MW of IT load, and be achievable in ~6 months for a moderate upgrade (since elements like chillers and transformers might already exist). Downtime risk is a concern: retrofitting a live facility means carefully sequencing work to avoid impacting running loads. There’s also the risk of stranded investment: if you pour money into retrofitting for 80 kW/rack but in 2 years need 150 kW, you might have to rip and replace again.
- Greenfield: designing a new facility (or new hall) optimized from the ground up for ultra-density and efficiency. This would include high-capacity power distribution (perhaps 415 V AC or 380 V DC distribution to racks), built-in liquid cooling (pumped loops to each rack, manifold infrastructure), and floor layouts that accommodate very heavy racks and possibly liquid infrastructure like coolant pumps, heat exchangers, etc. The benefit is you can hit full performance — e.g. 150 kW/rack, PUE ~1.10, no legacy constraints. The downside is lead time (12–24 months is common to plan and construct a new data hall) and cost. Perhaps a greenfield AI data hall at $10–12 million per MW of capacity, given the specialized cooling gear and higher redundancy typically desired. However, because you can pack more IT in less space, the net cost per GPU or per PFLOP can still be attractive — Schneider Electric found that 4× rack density could save ~14% on capital vs spreading the same load over more racks in a conventional setup introl.com. The improved efficiency (PUE savings) also yields ongoing OPEX benefit.
The decision often comes down to time-to-value vs long-term efficiency. A simple financial model: suppose a new Rubin-class GPU rack yields $X of revenue per day (from AI jobs). If a retrofit gets those GPUs online 6 months sooner than waiting for new build, that’s 6 months of X/day revenue banked — which could easily outweigh some energy inefficiency. On the other hand, if the operation will run for years, the energy savings of a custom-built efficient site (plus the ability to host more GPUs in the same building) can make it much more profitable in the long run.
For example, consider 1 MW of IT load (roughly 8 racks of Rubin-class). In an old site with PUE 1.30, the power overhead is 0.3 MW. In a new liquid-cooled site at PUE 1.10, overhead is 0.1 MW. At $0.10/kWh, that difference is about $175k per year in electricity cost saved per MW deployed. Over a 5-year server life, that’s $875k saved — nearly $1 million. Meanwhile, the capital to build new vs retrofit might differ by several million. If the new build costs $3M more, simple payback is ~3–4 years via energy savings alone. Any higher electricity price (e.g. in Europe at $0.20/kWh) doubles the savings, shortening payback. Thus, in power-expensive regions, greenfield builds often pencil out well. In low-cost power regions (or where one can secure cheap PPAs), the operational savings might not justify the wait — so retrofit and get computing running ASAP could be the call.
Downtime and risk: retrofitting often requires partial shutdowns — e.g. to tie in a new CDU skid to the chilled water loop, or to re-wire power panels for higher amperage. Operators must weigh the risk/cost of those disruptions (or the need to evacuate workloads temporarily) against the smoother start of a new site.
Stranded capacity: Greenfield builds have the luxury of being designed around the target load (say 200 kW racks). Retrofits might end up limited — e.g. you add liquid cooling but the old raised floor can’t handle >60 kW per rack due to airflow for the remaining air-cooled components, so you effectively “strand” some potential GPU density. The cost of that inefficiency (needing more racks or running GPUs at lower TDP to fit thermal limits) can be significant in opportunity terms.
A balanced strategy some adopt is a phased approach: retrofit a portion of current capacity to capitalize on immediate demand (and high cloud AI prices), while simultaneously building a new high-density wing for the next hardware generation. The retrofitted pods generate revenue to help fund the new build. Once the new hall is live, older space can either be decommissioned or repurposed for less demanding loads.
In any case, quantifying breakpoints is key. For a given electricity price, interest rate (cost of capital), and expected GPU utilization, one can compute the NPV of energy savings vs the NPV of earlier revenue from faster deployment. Additionally, one should consider the depreciation of GPUs — if GPUs are likely to be replaced or depreciated over 3 years, having them 6 months sooner yields 1/6th more useful life in that period. On the flip side, a rushed retrofit might underperform (if cooling is marginal, GPUs might throttle or run at reduced clock, effectively wasting some of their potential).
VII. Supply-Chain & Schedule Risk
Deploying a new GPU architecture isn’t just about the chips — it’s also about whether the supply chain can deliver all the pieces on time. With Rubin-class, a few supply risks loom large:
- Advanced Packaging Bottlenecks: The industry learned from Hopper/Blackwell that technologies like TSMC’s CoWoS (chip-on-wafer-on-substrate for HBM integration) can become a capacity bottleneck. In 2024, CoWoS substrate capacity was fully booked by AI chips, with TSMC expanding from ~16k wafers/month to 40k+, and still insufficient. For 2025, TSMC projects ~75k wafers/month CoWoS capacity, but demand (from Nvidia, AMD, etc.) might exceed that. Rubin-class GPUs will surely use advanced 2.5D packaging (if not CoWoS then an equivalent). There’s a non-zero risk that substrate shortages or packaging throughput could delay volume shipments. Similarly, the ABF substrates (the fiberglass resin boards HBM packages and GPUs sit on) have had supply constraints in recent years. Any slip in substrate supply could bottleneck an otherwise ready GPU. Mitigation: Nvidia and others are likely pre-paying and booking capacity years in advance (reports of Nvidia prepaying TSMC ~$5B for capacity were noted in previous gens). For data center operators, the mitigation is to stay flexible: plan for multi-vendor GPU SKUs if possible. If Nvidia Rubin GPUs slip due to packaging, alternative accelerators like AMD’s MI300X/MI350X series could fill in gaps (assuming they have different supply chain exposures).
- HBM4 Memory Ramp: HBM4 is cutting-edge DRAM, and only a couple of vendors (SK hynix, Micron, maybe Samsung) will supply it initially. Any yield hiccup or qualification issue can slow deliveries. SK hynix did showcase working HBM4 in April 2025, aiming for mass production by late 2025 — but volume ramp to meet thousands of GPUs worth of HBM could slip into 2026. If HBM4 isn’t ready in sufficient volume, Nvidia might have to fall back to a “Plan B” Rubin GPU that supports HBM3e. That would lower performance and complicate things like memory capacity plans. Operators should inquire about the memory configurations offered at launch — e.g. perhaps the first Rubin units might ship with HBM3e 36 GB stacks (still an upgrade from Blackwell, but not the full HBM4 jump).
- Optics and Networking Lead Time: Transitioning to 1.6 T networking will strain the optical transceiver supply chain. Many suppliers are just ramping 100 Gb/lane optics. Large orders for 1.6 T modules could have 30+ week lead times initially. Similarly, next-gen switches (51.2 T and 102.4 T) might be supply-constrained in 2025–26 due to both high demand and chip manufacturing limits. Any delay in networking gear availability could bottleneck full Rubin pod deployments (no point having GPUs if you lack the fabric to interconnect them at needed speed).
- Component Qualification & Firmware: New memory (HBM4), new PCIe standards, new NVLink, etc. all mean a lot of new firmware and software. There is a risk of early-life failures or performance bugs (e.g. initial Hopper systems saw some issues with HBM temps and needed firmware fixes). Schedule-wise, this is more of an operational risk: the hardware might arrive, but full performance and stability might take time (driver updates, BIOS tweaks, etc.).
- Lead times for facilities: Not to be overlooked, there’s also the schedule risk on the infrastructure side. Transformers, generators, and cooling units have lengthy lead times in the current environment (some >50 weeks for large switchgear). If you choose greenfield, ensure all long-lead facility items are ordered in parallel with GPU procurement. It’s little consolation to have racks of Rubin-class delivered if your new data hall isn’t ready due to a missing HV transformer or coolant pump. The supply chain for these industrial components has been tight, so plan accordingly (multi-source if possible, or pre-order standard sizes).
In summary, expect the unexpected: from substrate shortages to memory delays. The best operators will have a contingency plan — e.g. a staggered deployment where not all bets are on Rubin-class arriving exactly Q3 2025. Building in slippage in project plans (even just a quarter) is prudent. Also, consider diversification: maybe mix in some incrementally improved Blackwell systems or competitor GPUs as a hedge, so AI capacity growth isn’t halted if one product faces a setback.
Concluding….
The upcoming Rubin-class GPU architecture represents a Rubin’s vase of opportunity and challenge — what you see (unprecedented performance) comes hand-in-hand with what you might not immediately see (significant facility upgrades and cost inflection points). Looks like in order to to fully capitalize on Rubin-class silicon (with its multi-PFLOP compute and multi-terabyte memory), operators must have memory and fabric innovations arrive in tandem (HBM4 + 1.6T networking). Equally, those gains will only translate if your power and cooling can feed the beast; otherwise, one risks an expensive throttled installation.
So, retrofit or greenfield? If Rubin launched today, the answer might lean retrofit — to capture short-term AI demand. But looking at updated 2025 data on costs and timelines, a strong case emerges that true Rubin-class “AI factories” likely require greenfield designs, or at least deep refurbishment beyond a quick add-on. The breakpoints we quantified show that at scale, the efficiency and density benefits of purpose-built liquid-cooled facilities outweigh the upfront premium within a few years. Thus, organizations with large, sustained AI workloads should strongly consider new builds aligned with Rubin’s release. Conversely, for those dipping a toe or needing a stop-gap, retrofitting a few racks with advanced cooling can buy time and capacity — just go in with eyes open about the limitations.
In closing, the post-Blackwell jump will be an inflection point in data center architecture. We will see memory bandwidths once reserved for supercomputers become commonplace, and rack powers that redefine “high density.” The winners in this phase will be those who plan holistically: chip, systems, cooling, power, and economics. For hyperscalers and others making this leap, the best advice is to prepare early, stay agile with scenarios, and be ready to execute decisively once the Rubin-class curtain is lifted.
